content.body: Astrophysics applies physics to understand stars and the universe. Stars form from nebulae, and their life cycle depends on mass. The universe began with the Big Bang about 13.8 billion years ago.

Hubble's Law: v = H₀d
H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc (Hubble constant)

Star luminosity: L ∝ R²T⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann)
Distance modulus: m - M = 5 log(d/10pc)

content.title: Hubble's Law
content.body:
A galaxy is 100 Mpc away.
v = H₀ × d = 70 × 100 = 7000 km/s
This galaxy is receding from us at 7000 km/s

questions.question: Hubble's Law relates a galaxy's recession speed to its:
questions.explanation: v = H₀d — the farther the galaxy, the faster it recedes

questions.question: The Big Bang happened approximately:
questions.explanation: Current evidence places the Big Bang at approximately 13.8 billion years ago

questions.question: A main sequence star is powered by:
questions.explanation: Stars convert hydrogen to helium via nuclear fusion in their cores

questions.question: What is the final stage of a massive star (>8 solar masses)?
questions.explanation: Massive stars end as supernovae, leaving neutron stars or black holes

questions.question: Redshift of distant galaxies provides evidence for:
questions.explanation: Redshifted light shows galaxies moving away — the universe is expanding

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

lessons.title: Stars — Formation, Life Cycle and Classification

content.body: Stars are massive balls of plasma held together by gravity and powered by nuclear fusion. Our Sun is a medium-sized star. Stars are born from nebulae, evolve through different stages depending on their mass, and eventually die.

content.title: Star Life Cycle
content.body:
Low/medium mass stars (like Sun):
Nebula → Protostar → Main sequence (billions of years) → Red giant → Planetary nebula → White dwarf → Black dwarf

High mass stars:
Nebula → Protostar → Main sequence → Red supergiant → Supernova → Neutron star OR Black hole

Hertzsprung-Russell (HR) Diagram:
Plots luminosity vs surface temperature
Main sequence: most stars including Sun (diagonal band)
Giants and supergiants: top right
White dwarfs: bottom left
Hotter stars are bluer; cooler stars are redder

Stefan-Boltzmann for stars: L = 4πR²σT⁴
L = luminosity, R = radius, T = surface temperature

questions.question: What powers a star?
questions.explanation: Stars are powered by nuclear fusion — hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium, releasing enormous energy (E=mc²).

questions.question: What is the Sun classified as on the HR diagram?
questions.explanation: The Sun is a G-type main sequence star, sitting in the diagonal band of the HR diagram.

questions.question: What happens to a low-mass star after the main sequence?
options: Becomes a red giant then white dwarf
questions.answer: Becomes a red giant then white dwarf
questions.explanation: Low/medium mass stars (like Sun) expand into red giants, shed outer layers as planetary nebula, then cool as white dwarfs.

questions.question: A supernova can produce...
options: A neutron star or black hole
questions.answer: A neutron star or black hole
questions.explanation: Massive stars end their lives in supernova explosions, leaving behind either a neutron star or black hole.

questions.question: On the HR diagram, hotter stars appear...
questions.explanation: Wien's law: hotter stars emit peak radiation at shorter wavelengths — they appear blue/white.

questions.question: Stars form from...
questions.explanation: Stars begin as clouds of gas (mainly hydrogen) and dust that collapse under gravity to form protostars.

questions.question: Luminosity of a star depends on...
questions.explanation: L = 4πR²σT⁴. Luminosity increases with larger radius and much more rapidly with temperature.

questions.question: White dwarfs are...
options: Cool and small remnants of dead stars
questions.answer: Cool and small remnants of dead stars
questions.explanation: White dwarfs are the dense remnants of low-mass stars — Earth-sized but very hot initially, gradually cooling.

questions.question: What prevents main sequence stars from collapsing under gravity?
options: Outward radiation pressure from fusion balances gravity
questions.answer: Outward radiation pressure from fusion balances gravity
questions.explanation: Main sequence stars are in hydrostatic equilibrium: gravity inward = radiation pressure from fusion outward.

questions.question: Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 solar masses) determines...
options: Maximum mass of a neutron star
Maximum mass of a white dwarf
questions.answer: Maximum mass of a white dwarf
questions.explanation: If a white dwarf exceeds 1.4 solar masses, electron degeneracy pressure cannot support it — it collapses further.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Star life cycle depends on mass:
All stars: nebula → protostar → main sequence

Low mass (like Sun):
main sequence → red giant → planetary nebula → white dwarf → (black dwarf)

High mass:
main sequence → red supergiant → supernova → neutron star or black hole

Main sequence: H→He fusion, hydrostatic equilibrium
Hertzsprung-Russell diagram: luminosity vs temperature

content.title: Stellar Classification
content.body:
HR Diagram zones:
Main sequence: diagonal band (90% of stars)
Red giants: upper right (cool, luminous)
White dwarfs: lower left (hot, dim)
Supergiants: top (most luminous)

Spectral types O B A F G K M (hottest to coolest)
Oh Be A Fine Girl/Guy Kiss Me
Sun: G-type, surface T~5800K, mass=1M☉

content.title: Nucleosynthesis
content.body:
Main sequence: ⁴H → ⁴He (proton-proton chain)
Red giant: He → C, O (triple-alpha process)
Massive stars: C,O → Ne,Mg,Si,Fe
Iron-56: most stable — no more fusion possible → core collapse
Supernova: elements heavier than Fe formed and dispersed

questions.question: Main sequence star is powered by
questions.explanation: Main sequence: nuclear fusion of H→He in core, releasing energy that supports star against gravity

questions.question: Our Sun will eventually become a
questions.explanation: Sun (low mass): red giant → planetary nebula → white dwarf. Not massive enough for neutron star/black hole

questions.question: Supernova occurs for
options: high-mass stars when iron core collapses
questions.answer: high-mass stars when iron core collapses
questions.explanation: Massive stars: iron core (no more fusion) → collapses → rebound → supernova explosion

questions.question: Hydrostatic equilibrium means
options: gravity inward = radiation pressure outward (star stable)
questions.answer: gravity inward = radiation pressure outward (star stable)
questions.explanation: Main sequence balance: fusion energy pushes outward = gravity pulling inward → stable star

questions.question: White dwarf is held up by
questions.explanation: White dwarf: no fusion. Held against gravity by electron degeneracy pressure (Pauli exclusion principle)

questions.question: Heaviest elements (iron, gold, uranium) were formed
questions.explanation: Elements up to Fe in stellar cores; heavier elements during supernova explosions

questions.question: HR diagram plots
questions.explanation: Hertzsprung-Russell diagram: y-axis luminosity (or magnitude), x-axis surface temperature (right to left) or spectral type

questions.question: O-type stars are
options: the hottest, most luminous blue-white stars
questions.answer: the hottest, most luminous blue-white stars
questions.explanation: Spectral class O: T>30,000K, blue-white, most luminous, but rare and short-lived

questions.question: Nebula is
options: cloud of gas and dust from which stars form
questions.answer: cloud of gas and dust from which stars form
questions.explanation: Nebulae are stellar nurseries — gravity causes regions to collapse forming protostars

questions.question: Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4M☉) is maximum mass for a
questions.explanation: Above Chandrasekhar limit, electron degeneracy pressure cannot support white dwarf → collapses to neutron star

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

lessons.title: Cosmology: Big Bang and Expanding Universe

content.body:
Evidence for expanding universe:
Hubble's law: v = H₀d
H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc (Hubble constant)
Redshift: light stretched as source moves away
z = Δλ/λ = v/c (for v<<c)

Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB):
Remnant radiation from ~380,000 years after Big Bang
T≈2.7K, extremely uniform

Age of universe ≈ 1/H₀ ≈ 13.8 billion years

content.title: Big Bang Evidence
content.body:
1. Hubble's law: galaxies recede faster with distance
2. CMB: uniform 2.7K background radiation (Penzias & Wilson 1964)
3. Big Bang nucleosynthesis: predicted H:He ≈ 3:1 (observed!)
4. Large-scale structure: galaxy distribution matches models

FATE: Dark energy (68%), dark matter (27%), ordinary matter (5%)

content.title: Redshift and Blueshift
content.body:
Doppler effect for light:
Receding source: wavelength stretched → redshift (z>0)
Approaching source: wavelength compressed → blueshift
Andromeda galaxy: blueshifted (approaching Milky Way)
All other galaxies: redshifted (expanding universe)

questions.question: Hubble's law (v=H₀d) tells us
options: more distant galaxies recede faster → universe expanding
questions.answer: more distant galaxies recede faster → universe expanding
questions.explanation: Hubble's law: recessional velocity proportional to distance. All galaxies receding → universe expanding from all points

questions.question: Redshift of galaxy means
options: it is receding from us (light wavelength stretched)
questions.answer: it is receding from us (light wavelength stretched)
questions.explanation: Redshift: wavelengths of spectral lines longer than expected → Doppler effect → moving away

questions.question: Cosmic Microwave Background radiation is evidence for Big Bang because
options: it is the afterglow of the hot early universe, now cooled to 2.7K
questions.answer: it is the afterglow of the hot early universe, now cooled to 2.7K
questions.explanation: CMB: thermal radiation from when universe first became transparent (~380,000 years old), red-shifted to 2.7K today

questions.question: Age of universe estimated from Hubble constant as approximately
questions.explanation: t ≈ 1/H₀ ≈ 1/(70 km/s/Mpc) ≈ 13.8 billion years

questions.question: Dark energy makes up approximately what fraction of universe?
questions.explanation: Current model: 68% dark energy, 27% dark matter, 5% ordinary matter (baryonic)

questions.question: Hubble constant H₀ units are
questions.explanation: H₀ in km/s per megaparsec: velocity per unit distance. 1 Mpc ≈ 3.1×10²² m

questions.question: Olbers' paradox asks why the night sky is dark if universe is infinite. Resolution is
options: universe has finite age, so light from very distant stars hasn't reached us
questions.answer: universe has finite age, so light from very distant stars hasn't reached us
questions.explanation: Finite age → finite observable universe. We only see light from within the cosmological horizon

questions.question: Andromeda galaxy shows blueshift, meaning
options: it is approaching the Milky Way
questions.answer: it is approaching the Milky Way
questions.explanation: Blueshift indicates approach. Andromeda and Milky Way will collide in ~4.5 billion years

questions.question: Big Bang nucleosynthesis predicted the ratio of hydrogen to helium (3:1). This is considered strong evidence because
options: it was known before the theory
the prediction was made before observation and matched accurately
questions.answer: the prediction was made before observation and matched accurately
questions.explanation: Predictive success: Big Bang model predicted H:He ratio before it was precisely measured — strong scientific test

questions.question: What does z=0.5 redshift tell us about a galaxy?
questions.explanation: z=Δλ/λ≈v/c for small z. z=0.5: v≈0.5×3×10⁸=1.5×10⁸m/s, extremely distant galaxy

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Astrophysics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Astrophysics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Astrophysics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Astrophysics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Astrophysics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Astrophysics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Astrophysics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
